turns.content: I've been reading about default type parameters for structs and impl blocks, but I still don't fully grasp how they actually work in practice. I keep thinking that if I define a struct with T = String, then every instance of that struct will automatically hold a String value without me needing to specify the type at all time.

That's a common misconception when first encountering generics with defaults. While you can use default values like T = String in your struct definition to provide an initial state or builder pattern, the actual stored data is still typed as T until explicitly instantiated. The default only helps with convenience constructors rather than forcing all instances to be Strings automatically.

So I'm confused about whether the compiler will actually enforce T being a String in every case if I don't pass it? Does that mean my code might compile even if I try to use an int or float instead of a string?

You're right to question that assumption. The default value acts as a fallback for constructors but doesn't constrain what T actually is at runtime or in the type system itself. If you attempt to use an integer where your struct expects String, it will fail unless you explicitly define a conversion trait like From or implement some other mechanism to handle that specific scenario.
Okay so I need to be careful about passing the right types when creating instances? Is there a way to make this more flexible for different data types without losing the benefit of defaults?

Exactly, you should leverage traits like Clone or From to handle conversions gracefully. You can also consider using enums or union-like patterns with different type parameters if your use case requires true polymorphism rather than just a single default value.
I'm struggling with generics again. It reminds me of C++ templates, but I thought Rust required every type to be specified explicitly in the impl block unless it's a const generic.

You're right that C++ templates often require explicit instantiation, and Rust does support default generics like T = String. However, unlike C++, these defaults only apply to type parameters in the struct definition itself, not to all methods inside impl blocks unless you explicitly add them.
Ah okay so I can define a Foo<T = String> and then use it without specifying T everywhere. But does that mean every method in the impl block automatically gets the default too?

Not exactly, that's where the confusion lies. You can set defaults on struct fields like T = String, but methods still need explicit type parameters unless you use trait bounds or helper traits to constrain them.
So I'd have to write impl<T> Foo<T> for every method if I want flexibility?

Yes, you need to specify the generic in each impl block. However, many crates like serde_json::Value use this pattern by defining a struct with defaults and then providing specific implementations or trait bounds where needed.

I'm building a generic builder pattern in my project, but I keep getting confused about the difference between `struct Foo<T = String>` and just using `impl` blocks with default type parameters. Which one is actually supported by the compiler?

The syntax you are trying to use for struct fields like `struct Foo<T = String>` does not exist in standard Rust, so that approach will fail to compile. The correct way to define a generic struct with a default value is using `impl` blocks where you can specify the type parameter and its default value directly in the definition, such as `impl<T> struct MyStruct { ... }` followed by `MyStruct::default_type()`. If you want to set defaults for specific types like String or Vec<u8>, you need to use traits with associated functions rather than relying on default generic parameters within the struct definition itself. How are you planning to implement the builder logic? Do you want to provide a method that returns a `Foo` instance with an initial state, or do you need to construct it manually every time?
I see, so I can't just put `= String` inside the struct definition. But what if I define two separate structs instead of using generics? That seems like more boilerplate though.

You are correct that defining multiple specific structs is an option, but it creates unnecessary duplication and violates the DRY principle in Rust. The better approach is to use a trait with a default implementation or rely on the compiler's ability to infer types when possible. However, if you truly need a single generic struct that defaults to `String`, you should avoid putting constraints directly in the struct definition and instead provide a method like `fn new() -> Self` which returns an instance of your type with the default value already set. This keeps your code flexible while still providing convenient defaults for common cases.

Hey, I'm working on a config builder for our new tool and I keep trying to use the Json wrapper like a HashMap but it's complaining about missing types. It feels kind of like how Python dicts work where you don't have to specify every type manually, right?

That's exactly the right mental model! Rust doesn't do that with collections because it needs to know the exact size at compile time. You should look into struct Foo<T = String> which is a builder pattern example where T defaults to your base type so you don't have to write out the generic parameter every single time in impl blocks.
Oh, I see. So if I define my own struct with a default type, does that mean I can skip passing the type argument when calling methods on it?

Yes! That's the whole point. When you use impl blocks with default type params like impl Foo<T = String>, the compiler automatically fills in T if you don't provide it at call sites, which makes your builder API much cleaner and less verbose.
That sounds great but I'm still confused about how serde_json::Value interacts with this. Can a struct with default generics really work seamlessly with serialization?

Absolutely, that's exactly why these patterns are so common in the ecosystem. If you implement Serialize and Deserialize traits correctly on your generic struct with defaults, serde_json::Value will handle any underlying type automatically without you needing to re-specify types everywhere.
